stories.headline: Former CBI Scientist Missy Woods Pleads Guilty to Four Felonies in DNA Testing Scandal
stories.summary:
Yvonne 'Missy' Woods, a 65-year-old former Colorado Bureau of Investigation forensic scientist, pleaded guilty Tuesday to four felonies — cybercrime, perjury, attempting to influence a public servant, and forgery — in Jefferson County District Court. The plea deal will result in a sentence of between 8 and 16 years in prison, with the remaining 100 charges against her dismissed. Her sentencing is scheduled for September 8, and her previously scheduled jury trial has been canceled.

Woods mishandled DNA testing in at least 1,045 criminal cases during her 29-year career at the CBI, deleting, omitting, and manipulating data to speed up the testing process and boost her productivity. The cybercrime conviction stemmed from a pattern of entering false information into the CBI's computer system between 2008 and 2023, with costs to the state expected to exceed $11 million. The perjury conviction was tied to false testimony she gave during a 2014 jury trial.

First Judicial District Attorney Alexis King had originally charged Woods with 102 felonies in January 2025. The CBI said in a statement that Woods acted alone and that her conduct 'was not reflective of CBI's institutional practices or mission.' The agency said it has undertaken reforms aligned with national best practices in forensic science.

The case has sent shockwaves through Colorado's criminal courts, prompting reviews of hundreds of cases in which Woods' analysis played a role. Family members of victims whose cases were affected attended Tuesday's hearing, including relatives of a 1985 Lone Tree murder victim whose case was directly tied to Woods' work.

stories.headline: Aurora Establishes Office of Public Safety Accountability to Oversee Police and Fire Departments
stories.summary:
Aurora's City Council voted unanimously Monday night to create the Office of Public Safety Accountability, a new oversight body that will monitor the city's police and fire departments, 911 dispatch center, and detention facilities. The office could be operational by fall, with recruitment for an accountability manager beginning this summer, City Attorney Pete Schulte said.

The move comes after years of controversial policing practices in Colorado's third-largest city, including a series of fatal encounters between officers and unarmed Black men. It builds on a consent decree Aurora entered with the Colorado Attorney General's Office in 2021, following an investigation that found a pattern of racially biased policing and excessive force. The impetus for that investigation was the 2019 death of Elijah McClain, who was injected with a lethal dose of ketamine by paramedics after being stopped by police.

Under the new ordinance, any critical incident resulting in death or serious bodily injury must be reported to the accountability office within 30 minutes. The accountability manager will have access to all personnel and information involved in such incidents and must issue a report, as well as assign a family liaison within 48 hours and hold at least two community listening sessions per year.

Community advocates welcomed the move but noted its effectiveness will depend on the office's independence and transparency. Aurora joins Denver and Boulder among a handful of Front Range cities with formal independent oversight mechanisms for law enforcement.

stories.headline: Severe Thunderstorm Watch Covers Denver Metro and I-25 Corridor; Baseball-Size Hail Possible Overnight
stories.summary:
A Severe Thunderstorm Watch was in effect Tuesday night for the Denver metro area through the Colorado-Kansas state line until 1 a.m. Wednesday, with the National Weather Service warning that intense supercells could produce hail exceeding two to three inches in diameter — baseball to softball size. Damaging wind gusts of up to 80 mph and isolated tornadoes were also possible across the eastern half of the state.

Much of the I-25 corridor, including Fort Collins and the Denver metro area, was rated at a stage-three risk out of five, a higher threat level than many recent storm events. The National Weather Service specifically noted the threat includes the urban corridor. Storms were expected to move out of the state by 4 a.m. Wednesday.

Smoky skies from wildfires burning in Utah were also affecting air quality along the Front Range, with another hazy start expected Wednesday morning. Daytime highs were forecast to reach the mid-to-upper 80s Wednesday, with another round of late afternoon and evening storms possible. Red flag warnings remained in effect for mountain and high valley areas through much of the coming week.

A pattern shift is expected this weekend, with more sunshine, limited storm activity, and temperatures soaring into the mid-to-upper 90s Saturday and Sunday. Meteorologists said the warmer weather is expected to persist into early next week.

stories.headline: RTD to Run Bus Shuttles on A Line Sunday During Airport Rail Maintenance Window
stories.summary:
The Regional Transportation District will suspend A Line commuter rail service between Denver International Airport and the 61st-Peña station on Sunday for scheduled track maintenance. The work window runs from approximately 5:25 a.m. to 1:12 p.m., during which bus shuttles will replace train service on that segment. Regular A Line service between Denver Union Station and 61st-Peña will continue unaffected.

Passengers using the shuttle should plan for delays and allow extra travel time for airport connections, as buses will not follow regular train schedules. Boarding locations will be at Arrivals Gate 6 and Departures Gate 7 in the DEN Transit Center on Level 1, and at the front of 61st-Peña Station. The first train with continuous rail service to Denver Airport Station is scheduled to depart Union Station at approximately 12:30 p.m.

RTD said the maintenance is intended to improve the line's reliability and safety. Customers are encouraged to check the agency's Service Disruptions webpage and use the Next Ride web app for trip planning and real-time alerts.

stories.headline: 1Up Arcade Bar Closes LoDo Location After 15 Years, Plans Larger Lakewood Venue
stories.summary:
The 1Up Arcade Bar, which billed itself as Colorado's first arcade bar when it opened on March 23, 2011, permanently closed its original LoDo location at 1926 Blake St. on Monday, June 22. The closure was announced in anticipation of a new 13,000-square-foot location opening at 415 Teller St. in Lakewood's Belmar development, which owners described as the largest 1Up they have ever built.

Owners cited 'changing conditions in downtown Denver and the increasing financial pressures facing the hospitality industry' as reasons for leaving LoDo. The underground venue, a popular stop near Coors Field before and after Rockies games, helped pioneer the arcade-bar concept in Colorado and across the country. The new Lakewood location will occupy the former Lucky Strike space and feature an expanded collection of arcade games, pinball machines, redemption games, and other attractions.

The closure adds to a string of LoDo business shutdowns in recent weeks, including the Rock Bottom Restaurant & Brewery on the Sixteenth Street Mall and Church and Union on 17th Street. The 1Up's Colfax, Greenwood Village, and Westminster locations remain open. The company said it is proud to have helped pioneer gaming hospitality in Colorado and expressed confidence the Lakewood venue will carry on the brand's identity.

stories.headline: Rock Bottom Brewery, a Denver Institution for 35 Years, Closes Sixteenth Street Mall Location
stories.summary:
Rock Bottom Restaurant & Brewery, one of Denver's earliest microbreweries and the namesake of a national chain, has permanently closed its Sixteenth Street Mall location after 35 years in operation. A sign posted on the door of the 1001 Sixteenth St. venue Monday read: 'Unfortunately, we have permanently closed. Thank you for allowing us to serve the Downtown Denver community.'

Founded in 1991 by Boulder restaurateur Frank Day, Rock Bottom was among the first brewpubs in Denver and a feisty early competitor to Wynkoop Brewing. Day named the establishment as a cheeky reference to insurance giant Prudential's famous tagline, and the concept eventually expanded into a multi-state chain. Day died last year at age 93, and the company has changed private equity hands multiple times since he sold it in 2010.

The Denver closure follows the shuttering of Rock Bottom's Colorado Springs location in April and leaves the chain with just five locations nationally — three in Colorado, one in Illinois, and one in Boston. The closure comes as downtown Denver continues to struggle with foot traffic and economic headwinds facing the hospitality industry. Rock Bottom's departure, alongside that of the 1Up Arcade Bar and Church and Union, underscores ongoing challenges for LoDo businesses.

stories.headline: Denver Nightlife Licensing Overhaul Targeting 4 A.M. Bar Hours Wins Unanimous Committee Approval
stories.summary:
A Denver City Council committee unanimously approved a licensing overhaul Monday that moves the city closer to allowing bars and nightlife venues to serve alcohol until 4 a.m. The measure advances despite concerns raised by both venue operators and some council members about potential impacts on public safety and neighboring residents.

The proposal represents a significant shift in Denver's nightlife regulatory framework, which has long required last call at 2 a.m. in line with state law. Supporters argue the change would allow Denver to compete with other major cities and boost the local hospitality economy. The full council is expected to take up the measure in coming weeks.

stories.headline: Regency Centers Pays $37M for King Soopers-Anchored Retail Center in Highlands Ranch
stories.summary:
Regency Centers Corp. has acquired the Shops at Highland Walk in Highlands Ranch for $37.1 million, expanding the Florida-based retail real estate investment trust's footprint in the Denver metro area. The shopping center is anchored by a King Soopers grocery store, providing a stable tenant base for the property.

The purchase signals continued institutional investor interest in grocery-anchored retail properties in suburban Denver despite broader commercial real estate headwinds. Regency Centers operates a national portfolio of open-air shopping centers and has been actively expanding in the Denver market. Terms beyond the sale price were not disclosed.

stories.headline: Drought Forces Boating Restrictions, Water Shortages at Sylvan Lake and Rifle Gap State Parks
stories.summary:
Colorado Parks and Wildlife has enacted emergency water conservation measures and boating restrictions at Sylvan Lake State Park in Eagle County and Rifle Gap State Park in Garfield County, citing extreme drought conditions in Colorado's northwest corner. Both parks sit within areas the June 18 U.S. Drought Monitor classified as experiencing exceptional drought — the most severe category measured.

At Sylvan Lake, the park's primary water source, Zurcher Spring, has run completely dry with no signs of recovery. At Rifle Gap, historically low winter snowpack and early snowmelt have reduced reservoir levels enough that officials have cut motorized boat launching to a single lane and removed all courtesy docks. Hand-launched vessels such as kayaks, canoes, and stand-up paddleboards remain unaffected.

The restrictions highlight the broader water crisis facing Colorado amid ongoing drought and record heat. Parks and Wildlife officials urged visitors to check park conditions before traveling and to conserve water wherever possible.

stories.headline: Moose Attacks Hiker and Dogs on Trail Near Woodland Park; Wildlife Officials Issue Warning
stories.summary:
A hiker and their dogs were attacked by a moose on the Lovell Gulch Trail near Woodland Park at approximately 6:45 p.m. Monday, according to Colorado Parks and Wildlife. Paramedics evaluated the hiker at the scene and released them without serious injury; none of the dogs appeared to be hurt. Warning signs were posted in the area, though wildlife officers were not actively pursuing the animal as of Monday night.

The 5.3-mile loop trail, marketed as both kid- and dog-friendly on AllTrails, has had multiple recent hiker reports of moose and calves in the area. Wildlife officials noted that moose calves are born between mid-May and early June, making mothers particularly aggressive toward people and dogs well into summer, especially if approached or surprised.

Colorado Parks and Wildlife reminded hikers that moose can move at speeds up to 35 mph and should never be approached. Officials advised hikers to keep dogs on leashes, use alternate routes when moose are present, and watch for signs of aggression such as laid-back ears, a lowered head, or swaying behavior. If a moose charges, officials recommend running and placing a large object such as a boulder, car, or tree between yourself and the animal.

stories.headline: University of Denver Restructures Into Seven Colleges, Citing Enrollment Shifts and Funding Pressures
stories.summary:
The University of Denver is consolidating its 10 colleges and schools into seven as part of a broad restructuring intended to reduce administrative silos, expand interdisciplinary collaboration, and improve the institution's financial position. DU Chancellor Jeremy Haefner outlined the changes in a public statement, emphasizing that the reorganization is designed to strengthen the university's academic mission rather than signal contraction.

The restructuring builds on DU's existing strengths in psychology, counseling, and behavioral health while positioning the university to grow in the convergence of science, engineering, and health. Officials cited smaller incoming class sizes, shifting student interests, and changes in state and federal funding as structural challenges that cannot be addressed through fundraising or incremental programming adjustments alone.

DU's moves come amid a national wave of higher education restructuring. Since 2024, more than 297 colleges and universities across 47 states have announced cuts, layoffs, or closures. University officials said the process began nearly a year ago with input from students and faculty, who emphasized the value of interdisciplinary learning and reducing barriers between academic departments.

stories.headline: Colorado Primary One Week Out: Governor, Senate, and Statewide Offices on the June 30 Ballot
stories.summary:
Colorado voters have one week until the June 30 primary election, with races for governor, U.S. Senate, attorney general, and secretary of state on the ballot. Registered voters who have not mailed completed ballots must now use a drop box, a voter service center, or vote in person on Election Day, with polling places open from 7 a.m. to 7 p.m.

The most closely watched race is the Democratic gubernatorial primary, where U.S. Sen. Michael Bennet faces state Attorney General Phil Weiser. On the Republican side, State Sen. Barbara Kirkmeyer, State Rep. Scott Bottoms, and Victor Marx are competing. The winner of the general election in November will succeed Gov. Jared Polis, who is term-limited.

In the U.S. Senate Democratic primary, State Sen. Julie Gonzales — who would be Colorado's first female senator if elected — faces incumbent Sen. John Hickenlooper. State Sen. Mark Baisley is running unopposed in the Republican primary for that seat. Races for attorney general and secretary of state also feature competitive Democratic primaries, with Secretary of State Jena Griswold among those seeking the AG nomination.

weather.notable: Nearly 8 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: The Swiss have an interesting army. Five hundred years without a war. Pretty impressive. Also pretty lucky for them. Ever see that little Swiss Army Knife they have to fight with?

topStory.headline: Senate Passes War Powers Resolution Rebuking Trump Over Iran War, but Measure Carries No Legal Force
topStory.summary:
The U.S. Senate voted 50-48 on Tuesday to approve a concurrent resolution calling on President Donald Trump to halt military operations against Iran or obtain explicit congressional authorization before continuing any further action. The measure had previously passed the House of Representatives earlier this month by a vote of 215-208, marking the first time both chambers of Congress have approved a war powers resolution instructing a president to end a military action since the War Powers Resolution of 1973 was enacted.

Four Republican senators broke with their party to support the measure: Rand Paul of Kentucky, Lisa Murkowski of Alaska, Susan Collins of Maine, and Bill Cassidy of Louisiana. Democratic Sen. John Fetterman of Pennsylvania was the only member of his party to vote against it. Republican Sens. Mitch McConnell of Kentucky, who was hospitalized earlier this month, and Dave McCormick of Pennsylvania did not vote; White House officials noted that had both been present and voted in opposition, the resolution would have failed on a 50-50 tie.

Because the measure was introduced as a concurrent resolution rather than a joint resolution, it does not go to the president's desk for signature and carries no force of law. A White House official dismissed the vote as having "no significance" and argued it was moot in any case, saying hostilities had already ended with a ceasefire agreed on April 7. Trump responded on his Truth Social platform, calling the vote "poorly timed and meaningless" and referring to the four dissenting Republicans as "losers" who had provided "aid and comfort" to the enemy.

The United States entered the current conflict on February 28, when Trump joined Israel in launching strikes against Iran, triggering a broader regional war now approaching its fifth month. The conflict follows a separate episode in June 2025, known as the Twelve-Day War, in which Trump ordered bombers to strike three Iranian nuclear enrichment sites. Trump has maintained the military campaign is necessary to prevent Iran from developing a nuclear weapon, a stated goal Iran has denied pursuing.

Negotiations over the terms of a June 17 memorandum of understanding between the U.S. and Iran are ongoing in Switzerland, though the agreement's durability remains uncertain. Israel has continued to conduct strikes in Lebanon in apparent violation of the accord's terms, which called for a halt to fighting across all fronts. The Senate vote adds symbolic pressure on the White House ahead of November midterm elections, which will determine whether Republicans can maintain their slim majorities in both chambers of Congress.
